course_description:
This course is part of a suite of Literacy and English courses in the Master of Teaching (Primary) degree. Three interrelated aspects of the teaching profession provide a developmental framework for the courses: knowing yourself as a Literacy/English teacher; knowing your students; and knowing the disciplinary content of the subject English as well as the place of literacy teaching and learning within other curriculum areas. In this course, students will develop a repertoire of teaching strategies and practices related to the teaching of writing and children’s literature; identify a range of assessment techniques and strategies suitable for diverse contexts; and build their knowledge of key concepts related to literacy and the discipline of English.

EDUC7550 provides a comprehensive introduction to key aspects of the English learning area in the primary years of schooling. In the course, you will develop a repertoire of practices and strategies related to the teaching of writing and children's literature. You will identify and use a range of assessment techniques suitable for diverse contexts and build knowledge of key concepts related to the discipline of English. With the use of the Australian Curriculum: English, you will plan high-quality, intellectually rigorous writing activities and explore contemporary approaches for assessing student writing. Your completion of this course will be informed by key frameworks and approaches including the four resources model, the writing rope, and genre-based pedagogy. Strategies and resources utilised throughout EDUC7550, including embedded Information Communication Technologies (ICTs), are designed to enable you to demonstrate attainment of the Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (APST) at the graduate level. Specifically, the course's teaching and learning activities and assessment tasks target the following standards: 1.3, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.5, 3.1, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 4.1, 5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.4, 6.2, andﾠ6.4.

Additional timetable information
EDUC7550 is taught and assessed at Masters level. It is offered in a two hour lecture and one hour tutorial format.
course_aims: EDUC7550 ﾠaims to build your knowledge and skills in planning for, teaching, and assessingﾠwritingﾠand children's literature experiencesﾠin primary school English contexts.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate research-informed knowledge, understanding and analysis of literacy teaching and assessment strategies and their application in teaching the primary school English Curriculum in ways that enhance inclusive participation of all students including those from diverse backgrounds (linguistic, cultural, religious and socioeconomic). (APST: 1.3, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.5, 3.1, 3.3, 5.1)

Identify teaching strategies that include a range of verbal and non-verbal communication strategies and resources including ICTs that support inclusive student participation and engagement in English Curriculum primary classroom activities and learning. (APST: 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 4.1)

Demonstrate the capacity to effectively engage in assessment moderation and interpret student assessment data to evaluate student learning, provide timely and appropriate student feedback, and modify teaching practices to improve student learning. (APST: 5.2, 5.3, 5.4)

Demonstrate an understanding of the relevant and appropriate sources of professional learning for primary school English teachers and provide justification for continued professional learning in light of implications for improved student learning and teaching in the English Curriculum Learning Area. (APST: 6.2, 6.4)
Demonstrate clear, fluent, and coherent communication skills consistent with personal, professional, and academic conventions.

assessment_summary.title: Quality literature in-class tutorial task series
assessment_summary.due_date: Week 7 In-class Task 17/04/2026; Week 8 In-class Task 24/04/2026; Week 9 In-class Task 1/05/2026; Week 10 In-class Task 8/05/2026; To be held during tutorial time in weeks indicated.

assessment_details.title: Critical Reflection
assessment_details.task_description: For this task, you are required to write a critical reflection about your personal literacy skills and your professional responsibilities as a primary school teacher of English. Your critical reflection should be written to address the following three parts: · Part 1: Draw on the Australian Curriculum documents and the research literature to provide a definition of literacy and state its importance · Part 2: Draw on the Australian Curriculum documents and the research literature to explain teacher roles in developing primary school students’ literacy skills · Part 3: Reflect on your personal literacy skills and outline one (or more) key experience in your literacy development. Then, plan for your continuing professional development as a teacher of literacy. Consider professional organisations in primary English and more general groups as well as supportive policies and guidelines. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must submit the assessment electronically via the assignment link on the course website. By submitting your assignment, you are certifying that it is your original work and that is has not been previously submitted for assessment in any other course at university (unless resubmission is part of the requirements for the course assessment). You need to ensure you have completed UQ’s academic integrity module before you submit your assignment. The module can be found at: https://ww.uq.edu.au/integrity . Always keep a copy of your submitted assignment, and a receipt of the submission.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Assessing and Teaching Children's Writing
assessment_details.task_description: This assignment will enable you to develop your knowledge of teaching and assessing writing. While this is an independent task, you will engage in a moderation process with a partner for part of the assignment. The task is split into three parts. Part A: Assessing writing · To begin, you and your partner will select a sample of a primary school student’s writing as listed on Blackboard. Your sample may be an imaginative, informative, or persuasive text, and written in the context of a subject other than English (CC 2.4 – assessed). You should have the same sample as your partner if possible. · Separately from your partner, analyse your selected writing sample for its macro (structural) and micro (linguistic) choices. Drawing on information taught in the course and the Australian Curriculum: English , write annotations that identify strengths and weaknesses in the writing sample. · Prepare a short piece of written feedback for the student who wrote your writing sample, including positive comments and areas for improvement. Also, include a statement about the purpose of timely and appropriate feedback to students. Part B: Moderation · You and your partner will then moderate each other’s analysis, annotations, and student feedback. You may wish to change and improve your work in response to the moderation. · Keep a record of your discussions and draw on this to write a brief explanation about what your moderation process involved, and how it supported your judgements of student learning. Part C: Planning and assessing a writing activity · Using your analysed student writing sample from Part A, identify one macro (structural) or micro (linguistic) choice that could be improved the student’s writing and plan a suitable activity aimed at a small group of students with similar needs. · You will plan to teach a small group writing activity based on your identified macro or micro choice for improvement in the context of a subject other than English (CC 2.4 – assessed). · Select one Content Description from your student’s year level in the Australian Curriculum: English that relates to your identified choice for improvement. · Select a mentor text to support your writing activity. · For your writing activity, include: 1. One or more achievable yet challenging learning goal(s), communicated through learning intentions and success criteria in student-friendly language. 2. A description of how you will explicitly teach your identified macro (structural) or micro (linguistic) writing choice using the teaching and learning cycle, supported by your selected mentor text, and other appropriate resources including effective use of Information and Communication Technology. Your writing activity should include modelling and scaffolding processes that support how a student’s brain learns. Ensure your teaching strategies cater for the needs of students from diverse backgrounds. 3. An indication of how you will assess student learning. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Quality literature in-class tutorial task series
assessment_details.due_date: Week 7 In-class Task 17/04/2026 Week 8 In-class Task 24/04/2026 Week 9 In-class Task 1/05/2026 Week 10 In-class Task 8/05/2026 To be held during tutorial time in weeks indicated.
assessment_details.task_description: The in-class tutorial task series involve a number of written questions based on the content covered in our module on children’s literature. At the start of the tutorial for each of the weeks indicated, you will be read a picturebook and given access to select pages from the picturebook in an assessment task booklet. You will be asked questions that allow you to demonstrate your knowledge and understanding of key literature concepts including the text’s ideological messages (i.e., historical, social, cultural contexts), the use of literary concepts (i.e., character, theme, plot, point of view, and setting), and the use of semiotic tools to communicate meanings in the images. Questions will be short answer. Marks are earned through demonstrating correct answers to these questions. Each of the 4 tasks will be marked and your best 2 results will count towards your final grade for this assessment item. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
This assessment is completed in the tutorial sessions indicated. Your best two results from the four in-class tutorial tasks will count towards your final grade. If you are only able to attend two of the four tutorials, both of these tasks will count.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Students have four weekly opportunities throughout the semester to successfully complete two in-person tutorial tasks. As such, there is no provision for late submission. There is, however, double the amount of weekly opportunities in comparison to the number of required tasks, thus giving students ample opportunity to make up a week should they be sick or away.
In the event of disruption during the semester that prevents the scheduled assessment events occurring as planned (ie pandemic lockdown), the assessment will be changed to an alternative form. The timing of the assessment may also be impacted.
Late submission
These tasks need to be completed within your tutorial for secure assessment purposes. As such, there is no late submission avenue available.
